subject: Physics
q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> slides down the plane inclined at angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with an acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>g</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> . The value of coefficient of kinetic friction will be :
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> slides down the plane inclined at angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with an acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>g</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> . The value of coefficient of kinetic friction will be :
solution:
Applying Newton's second law along the direction perpendicular to inlined plane:

N-mgcos30∘=0 (As θ=30°)

(since the acceleration of block in the direction perpendicular to the inclined plane is 0)

∴N=mgcos30∘   .....(1)

Let μ be the coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the inclined plane. Applying Newton's second law along inclined plane:

mgsin30∘-μN=ma=mg4    ....(2)

(since the acceleration along the inclined plane,  a=g4)

∴mgsin30∘-μmgcos30∘=mg4   (from equation 1 and 2)

⇒mg2-32μmg=mg4

⇒μ=123

question: In the arrangement shown in figure a1,a2,a3 and a4 are the accelerations of masses m1,m2,m3 and m4 respectively. Which of the following relation is true for this arrangement?
solution:
The figure shows the tension acting on each string in the system.

∑T→·a→=0

⇒4Ta1+2Ta2+Ta3+Ta4=0

⇒4a1+2a2+a3+a4=0

q:
Three masses M=100 kg, m1=10 kg and m2=20 kg are arranged in a system as shown in figure. All the surfaces are frictionless and strings are inextensible and weightless. The pulleys are also weightless and frictionless. A force F is applied on the system so that the mass m2 moves upward with an acceleration of 2 ms-2. The value of F is

(Take g=10 ms-2)
question:
Three masses M=100 kg, m1=10 kg and m2=20 kg are arranged in a system as shown in figure. All the surfaces are frictionless and strings are inextensible and weightless. The pulleys are also weightless and frictionless. A force F is applied on the system so that the mass m2 moves upward with an acceleration of 2 ms-2. The value of F is

(Take g=10 ms-2)
solution:
Let acceleration of $100 \mathrm{~kg}$ block $=a_1$
FBD of $100 \mathrm{~kg}$ block w.r.t ground

$\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{N}_1=100 \mathrm{a}_1$ ... (i)
FBD of 20 block wrt $100 \mathrm{~kg}$

$\mathrm{T}-20 \mathrm{~g}=20(2)$
$\mathrm{T}=240$ ... (ii)
$\mathrm{~N}_1=20 \mathrm{a}_1$ ... (iii)
FBD of $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ block wrt $100 \mathrm{~kg}$

$10 \mathrm{a}_1-240=10(2)$
$\mathrm{a}_1=26 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
$\mathrm{~F}-240-20(26)=100 \times 26$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{F}=3360 \mathrm{~N}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : The limiting force of static friction depends on the area of contact and independent of materials.
Statement (II) : The limiting force of kinetic friction is independent of the area of contact and depends on materials.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : The limiting force of static friction depends on the area of contact and independent of materials.
Statement (II) : The limiting force of kinetic friction is independent of the area of contact and depends on materials.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
solution:
The limiting force of static friction depends on both the normal force and the coefficient of static friction, but it does not depend on the area of contact.

The force of kinetic friction is indeed dependent on the nature of the materials in contact and the normal force, but not on the area of contact.

Hence, Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.

q: A ball is thrown upward with an initial velocity <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> from the surface of the earth. The motion of the ball is affected by a drag force equal to <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi><msup><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> (where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is mass of the ball, <math><mi>v</mi></math> is its instantaneous velocity and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi></math> is a constant). Time taken by the ball to rise to its zenith is:
question: A ball is thrown upward with an initial velocity <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> from the surface of the earth. The motion of the ball is affected by a drag force equal to <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi><msup><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> (where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is mass of the ball, <math><mi>v</mi></math> is its instantaneous velocity and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi></math> is a constant). Time taken by the ball to rise to its zenith is:
solution:
Net force on ball,
F=-mg-mγv2
a=-g-γv2
dvdt=-g-γv2
∫v=V0v=0dvg+γv2=∫t=0t-dt
1gγtan-1γgvv=V0v=0=-t
∴t=1γgtan-1 γgV0

question: A satellite moving with velocity $v$ in a force free space collects stationary interplanetary dust at a rate of $\frac{d M}{d t}=\alpha v$ where $M$ is the mass (of satellite + dust) at that instant. The instantaneous acceleration of the satellite is
solution: $a_{\text {inst }}=\frac{\alpha \mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{M}}$

subject: Physics
q: A block A of mass 4 kg is placed on another block B of mass 5 kg, and the block B rests on a smooth horizontal table. If the minimum force that can be applied on A so that both the blocks move together is 12 N, the maximum force that can be applied on B for the blocks to move together will be :
question: A block A of mass 4 kg is placed on another block B of mass 5 kg, and the block B rests on a smooth horizontal table. If the minimum force that can be applied on A so that both the blocks move together is 12 N, the maximum force that can be applied on B for the blocks to move together will be :
solution: There is an error in this question. Given a smooth horizontal table, there would be no friction between the table and the lower (5 kg) block. As a result the "minimum" force on the upper (4kg) block for both blocks to move together would be 0 N.

solution:
T=5g⇒μ10+m≥5g⇒10+m≥50.15
⇒m≥23.33 kg⇒m=27.3 kg.

To do so, the person <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> pushes it at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the horizontal and requires a minimum force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math>, while the person <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> pulls the box at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the horizontal and needs minimum force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math>. If the coefficient of friction between the box and the floor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math>, the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is
question: A heavy box is to be dragged along a rough horizontal floor. To do so, the person <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> pushes it at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the horizontal and requires a minimum force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math>, while the person <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> pulls the box at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the horizontal and needs minimum force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math>. If the coefficient of friction between the box and the floor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math>, the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is
solution:
FAcos30°=μmg+FAsin30°

FBcos60°=μmg-FBsin60°

FAcos30°-μsin30°=μmg

FBcos60°+μsin60°=μmg

Divide the equation,

we get,

⇒FAFB=μmg/cos30°-μsin30°μmg/cos60°+μsin60°=23

q: Three blocks A, B and C are pulled on a horizontal smooth surface by a force of 80 N as shown in figure. The tensions T1 and T2 in the string are respectively:
question: Three blocks A, B and C are pulled on a horizontal smooth surface by a force of 80 N as shown in figure. The tensions T1 and T2 in the string are respectively:
solution:
Since, the length of the string will remain same. Hence, all blocks will have the same acceleration.

aA=aB=aC=F5+3+2=8010=8 m s-2

Therefore, T1=5×8=40 N.

Now,

T2-T1=3×8⇒T2=64 N

subject: Physics
q: Two blocks ($P$ and $Q$) with respectively masses $2$ kg and $1.5$ kg are joined by a massless thread. These blocks are mounted on a frictionless pulley which is fixed on the edge of a cube ($S$), as shown in the figure below. Block $P$ is positioned on the top surface which has no friction and block $Q$ is in contact with side-surface, having coefficient friction $\mu$. The cube ($S$) moves towards the right with acceleration of $\dfrac{g}{2}$, where $g$ is gravitational acceleration. During this movement the block $P$ and $Q$ remain stationary. The value of $\mu$ is _______. (take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: Two blocks ($P$ and $Q$) with respectively masses $2$ kg and $1.5$ kg are joined by a massless thread. These blocks are mounted on a frictionless pulley which is fixed on the edge of a cube ($S$), as shown in the figure below. Block $P$ is positioned on the top surface which has no friction and block $Q$ is in contact with side-surface, having coefficient friction $\mu$. The cube ($S$) moves towards the right with acceleration of $\dfrac{g}{2}$, where $g$ is gravitational acceleration. During this movement the block $P$ and $Q$ remain stationary. The value of $\mu$ is _______. (take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Let us analyze the forces acting on blocks $P$ and $Q$ in the non-inertial frame of reference of the cube $S$, which is moving to the right with an acceleration $a = \dfrac{g}{2} = 5 \text{ m/s}^2$.

In this frame, a pseudo force acts on both blocks towards the left.

For block $P$ (mass $m_P = 2 \text{ kg}$):
The forces in the horizontal direction are the tension $T$ in the string (acting towards the right) and the pseudo force $F_{pP}$ (acting towards the left). Since block $P$ is stationary relative to the cube and there is no friction on the top surface:
$T = m_P a = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ N}$

For block $Q$ (mass $m_Q = 1.5 \text{ kg}$):
The horizontal forces are the normal reaction $N$ from the side surface of the cube (acting towards the right) and the pseudo force $F_{pQ}$ (acting towards the left). Since block $Q$ is stationary horizontally relative to the cube:
$N = m_Q a = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5 \text{ N}$

The vertical forces acting on block $Q$ are the downward gravitational force $m_Q g$, the upward tension $T$, and the frictional force $f$.
The downward force is $m_Q g = 1.5 \times 10 = 15 \text{ N}$.
Since $m_Q g > T$ ($15 \text{ N} > 10 \text{ N}$), the block has a tendency to slide downwards, so the static friction $f$ must act upwards.
For vertical equilibrium of block $Q$:
$T + f = m_Q g$
$10 + f = 15 \Rightarrow f = 5 \text{ N}$

To keep the block stationary, the required friction must be less than or equal to the maximum static friction ($f \le \mu N$). Assuming the limiting case to find the value of $\mu$:
$\mu N = f$
$\mu (7.5) = 5$
$\mu = \dfrac{5}{7.5} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.67$

Answer: $0.67$

q: A block kept on a rough inclined plane, as shown in the figure, remains at rest upto a maximum force 2N down the inclined plane. The maximum external force up the inclined plane that does not move the block is 10N. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the plane is: [Take g=10 m/s2 ]
question: A block kept on a rough inclined plane, as shown in the figure, remains at rest upto a maximum force 2N down the inclined plane. The maximum external force up the inclined plane that does not move the block is 10N. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the plane is: [Take g=10 m/s2 ]
solution:
2+mgsin⁡θ=μ mgcos⁡θ

mgsin⁡θ-μ mgcos⁡θ=2 ........1
mgsin⁡θ+ μ mgcos⁡θ=10 .......2

equation (1) devided by (2)

mg sin⁡θ-μcos⁡θmg sin⁡θ+ μcos⁡θ=-210=-15 

1-μ35=-1+μ3

6=43 μ

μ=32

subject: Physics
q: The normal reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> for a vehicle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> mass, negotiating a turn on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> banked road at maximum possible speed without skidding is ____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: The normal reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> for a vehicle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> mass, negotiating a turn on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> banked road at maximum possible speed without skidding is ____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
At vmax, f will be limiting in nature.

∴ Balancing force in vertical direction, Ncos30°-mg-μNcos60°=0
⇒Ncos30°-μcos60°=mg
∴ N=800×10(0.87-0.1)≈10.2×103 kg m s-2
Hence, option 1.

subject: Physics
q: A $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass is suspended from the ceiling by a rope of length $4 \mathrm{~m}$. A horizontal force ' $F$ ' is applied at the mid point of the rope so that the rope makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with respect to the vertical axis as shown in figure. The magnitude of $F$ is : (Assume that the system is in equilibrium and $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
question: A $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass is suspended from the ceiling by a rope of length $4 \mathrm{~m}$. A horizontal force ' $F$ ' is applied at the mid point of the rope so that the rope makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with respect to the vertical axis as shown in figure. The magnitude of $F$ is : (Assume that the system is in equilibrium and $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}_1 \sin 45^{\circ}=\mathrm{F} \\ & \mathrm{T}_1 \cos 45^{\circ}=\mathrm{T}_2=1 \times \mathrm{g} \\ & \therefore \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{g}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{F}=10 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

question:
The figure represents the momentum time (p-t) curve for a particle moving along an axis under the influence of the force. Identify the regions on the graph where the magnitude of the force is maximum and minimum respectively ?

If t3-t2<t1
solution:
From Newton's second,

F=dpdt=slope of p-t curve

From the given graph, we can see that slope of region c is maximum and slope of region b is minimum.

Thus, Fc>Fa>Fb.

q: A block starts moving up an inclined plane of inclination <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with an initial velocity of<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. question: A block starts moving up an inclined plane of inclination <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with an initial velocity of<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. It comes back to its initial position with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of the coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the inclined plane is close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>1000</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> The nearest integer to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is :
solution:
A to B

a1=g sin 30°+μg cos 30°

=g2+μg32 ; g=10 m s2

v02-2a1s=0

s=v02a1    .....(i)

B to A

a2=g2-μ32g

V022=2a2s

s=V024a2  .....(ii)

From equation (i) and (ii)

V02a1=V024a2

⇒   a1=4a2

⇒   5+53μ=45-53μ

⇒   5+53μ ⇒25 3μ=15  ⇒  μ=35=0.346=3461000

So, 11000=3461000

q: A balloon has mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> in air. The air escapes from the balloon at a uniform rate with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The air escapes from the balloon at a uniform rate with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Then, the average force acting on that balloon will be (in dyne).
solution:
Given that the mass of the air m=10 g and it escapes in time t=5 s with velocity v=4.5 cm s-1.

The average force on the balloon can be given as,

F=vdmdt

⇒F=4.5×105=9 dyne

q: A marble block of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ lying on ice when given a velocity of $6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is stopped by friction in $10 \mathrm{~s}$. Then the coefficient of friction is
question: A marble block of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ lying on ice when given a velocity of $6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is stopped by friction in $10 \mathrm{~s}$. Then the coefficient of friction is
solution:
$a=\mu g=\frac{6}{10}$             [ using $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{u}+\mathrm{at}$ ]
$\Rightarrow \mu=\frac{6}{10 \times g}=\frac{6}{10 \times 10}=0.06$

subject: Physics
q: A rocket is fired vertically from the earth with an acceleration of 2g, where g is the gravitational acceleration. On an inclined plane inside the rocket, making an angle <math><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math> with the horizontal, a point object of mass m is kept. The minimum coefficient of friction <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></msub></math> between the mass and the inclined surface such that the mass does not move is:
question: A rocket is fired vertically from the earth with an acceleration of 2g, where g is the gravitational acceleration. On an inclined plane inside the rocket, making an angle <math><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math> with the horizontal, a point object of mass m is kept. The minimum coefficient of friction <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></msub></math> between the mass and the inclined surface such that the mass does not move is:
solution:
3mgsin⁡θ=μ3mgcos⁡θ

μ=tan⁡θ

question: A player caught a cricket ball of mass $150 \mathrm{~g}$ moving at a rate of $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If the catching process is completed in $0.1 \mathrm{~s}$, the force of the blow exerted by the ball on the hand of the player is equal to
solution:
$(m v-0) \Rightarrow 0.15 \times 20$
$\mathrm{F}=\frac{3}{0.1}=30 \mathrm{~N}$

subject: Physics
q: Four forces are acting at a point P in equilibrium as shown in figure. The ratio of force F1 to F2 is 1:x where x=_____ .
question: Four forces are acting at a point P in equilibrium as shown in figure. The ratio of force F1 to F2 is 1:x where x=_____ .
solution:
Here, θ=45°

Applying equilibrium conditions,

Taking components along x & y

Considering right side positive, ∑Fx=0

F1+1sin45°-2sin45°=0F1=2sin45°-1sin45°

F1=2-12=2-12=12

Taking upward positive,∑Fy=0

1cos45°+2cos45°-F2=0F2=1cos45°+2cos45°

F2=2+12=2+12=32

F1:F2=1:3

x=3

subject: Physics
q: A light string passing over a smooth light pulley connects two blocks of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ (where $m_2>m_1$ ). If the acceleration of the system is $\frac{g}{\sqrt{2}}$, then the ratio of the masses $\frac{m_1}{m_2}$ is:
question: A light string passing over a smooth light pulley connects two blocks of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ (where $m_2>m_1$ ). If the acceleration of the system is $\frac{g}{\sqrt{2}}$, then the ratio of the masses $\frac{m_1}{m_2}$ is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & a=\left(\frac{M_2-M_1}{M_1+M_2}\right) g \\ & \frac{g}{\sqrt{2}}=\left(\frac{M_2-M_1}{M_1+M_2}\right) g \\ & \left(M_1+M_2\right)=\sqrt{2} M_2-\sqrt{2} M_1 \\ & \frac{M_1}{M_2}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}-1}{\sqrt{2}+1}\right)\end{aligned}$

q: Two fixed frictionless inclined plane making an angle $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ with the vertical are shown in the figure. Two block $A$ and $B$ are placed on the two planes. What is the relative vertical acceleration of $A$ with respect to $B$ ?
question: Two fixed frictionless inclined plane making an angle $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ with the vertical are shown in the figure. Two block $A$ and $B$ are placed on the two planes. What is the relative vertical acceleration of $A$ with respect to $B$ ?
solution:
$m g \sin \theta=m a$
$\therefore \quad a=g \sin \theta$
where $\mathrm{a}$ is along the inclined plane
$\therefore \quad$ vertical component of acceleration is $\mathrm{g} \sin ^2 \theta$
$\therefore \quad$ relative vertical acceleration of $A$ with respect to $B$ is

subject: Physics
q: Three identical blocks of masses $\mathrm{m}=2 \mathrm{~kg}$ are drawn by a force $\mathrm{F}=10.2 \mathrm{~N}$ with an acceleration of $0.6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ on a frictions surface, then what is the tension (in $\mathrm{N}$ ) in the string between the blocks $B$ and $C$?
question: Three identical blocks of masses $\mathrm{m}=2 \mathrm{~kg}$ are drawn by a force $\mathrm{F}=10.2 \mathrm{~N}$ with an acceleration of $0.6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ on a frictions surface, then what is the tension (in $\mathrm{N}$ ) in the string between the blocks $B$ and $C$?
solution:
Apply Newton's second law
$$
\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{ab}}=m a ; \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{ab}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{bc}}=\mathrm{ma} \quad \therefore \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{bc}}=7.8 \mathrm{~N}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The minimum velocity (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ ) with which a car driver must traverse a flat curve of radius $150 \mathrm{~m}$ and coefficient of friction $0.6$ to avoid skidding is
question: The minimum velocity (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ ) with which a car driver must traverse a flat curve of radius $150 \mathrm{~m}$ and coefficient of friction $0.6$ to avoid skidding is
solution: The condition to avoid skidding, $\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\mu \mathrm{rg}}=\sqrt{0.6 \times 150 \times 10}=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q: Two point masses of mass $m_1=f M$ and $m_2=(1-f) M(f < 1)$ are in outer space (far from gravitational influence of other objects) at a distance $R$ from each other. They move in circular orbits about their centre of mass with angular velocities $\omega_1$ for $m_1$ and $\omega_2$ for $m_2$. In that case
question: Two point masses of mass $m_1=f M$ and $m_2=(1-f) M(f < 1)$ are in outer space (far from gravitational influence of other objects) at a distance $R$ from each other. They move in circular orbits about their centre of mass with angular velocities $\omega_1$ for $m_1$ and $\omega_2$ for $m_2$. In that case
solution: Angular velocity is the angular displacement per unit time i.e., $\omega=\frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t}$ Here $w_1=w_2$ and independent of $f$.

subject: Physics
q: A $0.5$ kg mass is in contact against the inner wall of a cylindrical drum of radius $4$ m rotating about its vertical axis. The minimum rotational speed of the drum to enable the mass to remain stuck to the wall (without falling) is $5$ rad/s. The coefficient of friction between the drum's inner wall surface and mass is _______. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: A $0.5$ kg mass is in contact against the inner wall of a cylindrical drum of radius $4$ m rotating about its vertical axis. The minimum rotational speed of the drum to enable the mass to remain stuck to the wall (without falling) is $5$ rad/s. The coefficient of friction between the drum's inner wall surface and mass is _______. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
The normal force $N$ provides the necessary centripetal force for the mass to move in a circle:

$N = m \omega^2 R$

For the mass to remain stuck to the wall without falling, the upward frictional force must balance the downward gravitational force:

$f \ge mg$

Since the maximum static friction is $f_{max} = \mu N$, we have:

$\mu N \ge mg$

$\mu (m \omega^2 R) \ge mg$

$\mu \ge \dfrac{g}{\omega^2 R}$

Substituting the given values $g = 10$ m/s$^2$, $\omega = 5$ rad/s, and $R = 4$ m:

$\mu \ge \dfrac{10}{5^2 \times 4}$

$\mu \ge \dfrac{10}{100}$

$\mu \ge 0.1$

The minimum coefficient of friction is $0.1$.

Answer: $0.1$

q: As shown in figure, a 70 kg garden roller is pushed with a force of F→=200 N at an angle of 30° with horizontal. The normal reaction on the roller is (Given g=10 m s-2)
question: As shown in figure, a 70 kg garden roller is pushed with a force of F→=200 N at an angle of 30° with horizontal. The normal reaction on the roller is (Given g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Component of the force perpendicular to the ground,

Fsin30°.

Now for equilibrium along vertical direction, we get

N=mg+Fsin30°=70×10+200×12=800 N

q: A curved in a level road has a radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum speed of a car turning this curved road can be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> without skidding. If radius of curved road is changed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the coefficient of friction between the tyres and the road remains same, then maximum allowed speed would be _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A curved in a level road has a radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum speed of a car turning this curved road can be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> without skidding. If radius of curved road is changed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the coefficient of friction between the tyres and the road remains same, then maximum allowed speed would be _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
The maximum speed is given as v=μgr, where, μ is coefficient of friction and r is radius of curved road.

⇒v1v2=r1r2

⇒30v2=7548=2516=54

⇒v2=24 m s-1

question: A spaceship in space sweeps stationary interplanetary dust. subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ is at rest at the origin at time $\mathrm{t}=0$. It is subjected to a force $\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{F}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$ in the $x$ direction. Its speed $v(t)$ is depicted by which of the following curves?
question: A particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ is at rest at the origin at time $\mathrm{t}=0$. It is subjected to a force $\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{F}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$ in the $x$ direction. Its speed $v(t)$ is depicted by which of the following curves?
solution:
$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{F}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_0}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$ 
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_0}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$ 
$\quad\int \mathrm{dv}=\int_0^{\mathrm{t}} \frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}} \mathrm{dt}$
$\Rightarrow v=\frac{F}{m}\left[\frac{-1}{b}\right]\left[e^{-b t}\right]_0^t$
$\Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{F}{m b}\left[e^{-b t}\right]$
$v=0 \text { at } t=0$
and $\quad v \rightarrow \frac{F}{m b}$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$
So, velocity increases continuously and attains a maximum value of $v=\frac{F}{m b}$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$.
